draft.introduction: The Apostolic Tradition (c. 215 CE), attributed to Hippolytus of Rome, is the most important source for early Christian liturgical practice. It describes the ordination of bishops, the catechumenate, and the eucharistic prayer in remarkable detail.

translation.text: We give you thanks, O God, through your beloved child Jesus Christ, whom in the last times you sent to us as savior and redeemer and messenger of your will, who is your inseparable Word, through whom you made all things, and in whom you were well pleased. You sent him from heaven into the Virgin’s womb; he was made flesh and was manifested as your Son, being born of the Holy Spirit and the Virgin.
translation.original_text: [Reconstructed from Latin, Coptic, Ethiopic, and Arabic versions]

translator_notes.note: This eucharistic prayer is the oldest surviving anaphora (eucharistic prayer) in Christianity. Its structure—thanksgiving for creation, narration of salvation history, institution narrative, epiclesis—became the template for all subsequent eucharistic liturgies in both East and West. The original language is disputed (Greek or Latin), and the text survives only in derivative versions.
